item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this concept of SCP that goes into other fictional media, no matter if it is a movie, cartoon, videogame, stage play… And the way more tests are made keeps the concept fresh for new possibilities.

Too bad I didnt see mention of any Yoko Taro game, bad things usually happen to everyone.

Unless…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this! The way the article is written is proof that you can write a humorous peice which by context alone suggests a somewhat horrifying desire for total control.
Also… for Saló… I looked through the comments here so I hope I didn't miss this being brought up, but it sounds a lot to me like the captives here are being "bathed in His Tears" from -093. Is this just me reading too far into things? I'm new here and hope this is an okay comment to make. Either way, it's greatly written!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly one of the most unique ones i've seen

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean, plasma weapon is plasma weapon. It's weapon that hurls plasma i.e. ionized gas, a valid scientific nomenclature. It's not like it's saying "magic cannon" or something, and even if it was, this is the Foundation. Magic exist.

Energy weapon, or more accurately "directed energy weapon" is real thing, and mentioning it doesn't make the article any less "scientific". Microwave, laser, and sonic weapon could all be categorized as directed energy weapon, as could particle beam weapons. I think the US had even field-tested some prototype infrared laser weapon.

Also yeah, what Malice said, it's a fictional piece even in-universe. There's no need to over explain it, cuz the Foundation doesn't know and doesn't care.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can't really think of a more clinical way to describe them than "energy-based weaponry" and "plasma-based weaponry". They are fictional, after all; the Foundation wouldn't realistically need to describe them in any more detail than the average sci-fi writer would.

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: iDhagu!, instead of replying to your own comments, please edit your previous post using the "edit" function under the "options" tab to the lower right of every comment. That prevents spam buildup, and it's in the rules.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Other than that, this is a funny and enjoyable SCiP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Plasma weapons and high-energy rifles?
What kind of Sci-fi bullshit is that. Use actual clinical english to describe their weaponry.
cool

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Said SCP-3922-A instances are equipped with rocket launchers."

I flipping lost it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, If you really think about it, the Three Moons people maybe the most tyrannical force there is since it's going out of their way to insert themselves into fictional narratives to enforce laws on fictional characters and killing fictional tyrants. +1 for the absurdity of the idea and for indirectly showing just how scary the Three Moons can be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is outright satire of would be "cultural police" bitching about wrongthink in entertainment media. Love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I +1'd just for TACTICAL UNDONKIFICATION ORDINANCE.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea is really cool. I also think that it's really cool that the intensity of the test results are directly correlated to the movie ratings in such a way that Salò can be used as a payoff.

If I ever have to refer to Salò in a good light ever again though, I will personally kill you. I had forgotten about that movie for years until I saw that title mentioned in the logs, and the look of shock I had was worthy of a 2008 reaction video.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that I viscerally just really don't like this. I can't ever tell with the three moon stuff what's irony and what's supposed to be taken seriously, and it really makes me uncomfortable. That so many other readers seem to really like it just amplifies my discomfort multiplicatively. Which may be the intent… in which case, well done Dave, have a downvote. :|

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This really needs an extended test log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My only regret is that I have but one vote to give. +1 for the article, +1,000,000 in spirit for "TACTICAL UNDONKIFICATION ORDINANCE".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My Neighbor Totoro

The movie proceeds as normal until Mei runs away from the house. Three Moon agents swiftly locate and return her to the house after giving her a lecture on proper behavior. The Totoro instances, due to their rarity, are tranquilized, tagged, fitted with radio collars and then released back into the wild for later monitoring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: xD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is sum wered shit

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Lampchop's Singalong

The show proceeds as normal until the end, where 'This is the song that never ends' is played. The agents then bring a halt to the show and arrest the adults for introducing a low level memnetic hazard. The children are hustled off to therapy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My Neighbor Totoro would probably just have a Three Moons police presence in a few background scenes, while in the Land of Make-Believe, they’d atomize Lady Elaine Fairchilde and her reality-bending boomerang, which was clearly stolen from the Chaos Insurgency.

…this would be off-screen, of course, and a Three Moons representative would come along and say that “Lady Fairchilde couldn’t make it today” or something, while you can hear her screams muffled by plasma rifles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mr.Rogers notices the Instances arguing about whether or not 'Land of Make Believe' has drug connections. Mr.Rogers invites the Instances to have a friendly debate about their conflict and the issues are resolved with everyone shaking hands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Presumably nothing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What would happen if Mr.Roger's Neighborhood and My Neighbor Totoro were used?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I WANT THIS FOR REAL
+1

item.title: Re: On the significance of "Salò"
item.content:encoded:
Without the Corbenic works addressing that hypocrisy, or questioning whether the end here justifies the means, it's not clear that there is an understanding that the similarity exists at all.

Also, you can't make a good faith argument that torture is justified by good intentions. Even if we accept that fictional characters have sufficient reality and agency to be responsible for their fictional crimes, how does their torture (without trial) make the world a better place (as opposed, for example, to trial and imprisonment)?

item.title: Re: On the significance of "Salò"
item.content:encoded:
I think the hypocrisy is intentional, though.

The big difference, honestly, is intention. The three moons guys do it cuz they wanna make the world a better place, whereas, say, the Masters in Salo do it just for the sake of doing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It’s the film history buff equivalent of blue waffle, or some equally horrifying shock site. Let’s just say unless you’re a film history buff yourself, you’re better off taking my word for it.

item.title: Re: On the significance of "Salò"
item.content:encoded:
[the masters] use power to destroy their captives … They created a dark, disgusting place where power is used to torture and destroy…

[Three Moons' response is that the masters] will spend the rest of eternity in incomprehensible, unceasing pain.

the masters are the antithesis to everything the Three Moons stands for

This argument does not compute for me. Three Moons sounds less like the antithesis of the masters than the exact same thing by another name. Both groups use their position of power to torture others.

This has always been my fundamental issue with the Corbenic skips as a whole. The Three Moons / Corbenic infrastructure is presented as morally better than the Foundation (at least in 2922, and arguably throughout), but it appears to be a Kafka-esque totalitarian nightmare with incomprehensible rules. I think if more was done to present a human reaction to that (from the humans in Corbenic) and explore the consequences, I might be more engaged with the story.

item.title: On the significance of "Salò"
item.content:encoded:
Figured I'd rise from the grave to offer my thoughts on this one, as I've been browsing Series IV and this is one of my favorites. (WARNING: This analysis discusses Salò in detail, and that movie is NOT for the faint of heart).

+ show block
– hide block

So the surface-level interpretation is easy enough: the Three Moons come up with the most horrible torture possible for the some of the most depraved content ever put on cinema. Salo contains violent and gruesome depictions of sexual and psychological torture, including (but not limited to) anal rape, forced consumption of feces, involuntary satanic "weddings"…you get the idea.

Three Moons sees this, and decides the "Masters" are the perfect ones to make an example out of. But there's more to it than that.

There are two main themes in Salò: sexual violence as a form of power, and the anarchy that power creates. The latter is the one important to this SCP. There is a scene in the film where a master says something along the lines of "We Fascists [movie is set in 1944 Nazi-occupied Italy] are the truest anarchists, as only we see the true anarchy that power creates."

In other words, the masters are the antithesis to everything the Three Moons stands for. They use power to destroy their captives, and to create a world where there is no rules: Orwell's boot, in its most graphic and violent form.

Three Moons saw the masters and didn't just say "these men need to be punished for breaking the law and committing sadistic acts of torture." They said "These men need to be punished because people need to know we are not fucking around. You do NOT get away with this shit in life and go unpunished for it in death." And "this shit" refers not only to the acts of torture and rape themselves, but the philosophy they used to justify it.

They created a dark, disgusting place where power is used to torture and destroy. The best way for the Three Moons to punish them would be to show the viewer that, if you dedicate your life to using your power to create anarchy, you will spend the rest of eternity in incomprehensible, unceasing pain.

"YOU HAVE BEEN WARNED".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Could someone explain the plot to me

No. Nobody can explain the plot to you. This is not a joke answer, it's the literal truth. I don't think even Pasolini could have explained the plot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I first saw Saló on accident when I was 12 and it scared me to death and caused a disgust that I can't quite describe. I watched the entire movie out of some morbid disbelief and also an equally morbid interest to figure out what the hell was going on. The version I watched had no subtitles and I know solely English, although I was old enough to recognize the rape and torturous scenarios being shown to me. I also understood that the four "masters" both orchestrated this and held some positions of power in society, and undertook the "120 days of sodom" to act on their darkest, most disgusting sexual/power fantasies, however I'm plagued with spaces of information that I can't intuitively fill. What was the role of the elderly, strangely dressed women? They seemed almost excited at the location and uncaring of what was done to the children. Why were the guards so chill with the job, and why were they all very specifically young and fit men? And the guards at the end after the brutal executions happen, the dance and kiss. Why?

Could someone explain the plot to me, and if not here, in a PM perhaps? I was randomly reading and saw the Saló reference and it all kinda flooded back to me and my mind is now littered with questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Popping out of my usual lurker mode to ask if there's any chance for a separate document with more tests? Something like a: "See document SCP-3922-2" deal.

I've always been a sucker for that kinda thing.

Regardless this scip is now among my favorite's, I think DalaiLlama said it best:

The mental image of Combine soldiers storming Pleasure Island and making sure everything fits the film rating is hilarious to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's that movie? I fear for my search history in case is anything specially bad.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: I'm willing to bet you own the appropriate software to grant you usage rights on that font. Gonna call this one compliant.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Sorry this comes so late: Copperplate Gothic Bold.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: What is the font you used? If you used any of the Fonts available in Windows, for example, you'd be fine (as long as they don't end up in a PDF anyway).

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded:
I made it. The moons were a white circle with a smaller, black circle over it, which I then copied three times and aligned.

As for the font, I just chose whichever would look more official.

If necessary, I'd be more than happy to alter the image.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Would it be all right if I just changed the font? If so, which ones are okay

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Obviously.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded:
Important note:

+ Typefaces have a complicated relationship with copyright.
- Typefaces have a complicated relationship with copyright.

In pretty much any country except Russia you can use whatever font you want for whatever you want- the protections are entirely for the typeface as a whole.

Typefaces don't actually qualify for copyright at all in the US and Japan- you have to file a design patent. The only automatic protections are for digital vector fonts because TrueType/OpenType scripting technically makes them computer programs. The letter shapes themselves aren't protected.

Even in countries where the letter designs are automatically protected, they tend to only have copyright in the sense that you can't make a Totally-Not-Traced-Papyrus font and sell it and you can get sued for restitution if it turns out your license didn't cover mass printing or something. Even that protection only lasts a couple years unless you get a design patent.

Hasbro, for instance has a font company suing them over alleged infringement with a font used for a lot of MLP stuff. It wouldn't be a problem if they had just created their own knockoff of the font; the lawyer-summoning part is entirely about whether the company has committed software piracy by using unlicensed copies of the font file.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded:
Images below the threshold of originality are not unprotected by law. They're just unprotected by copyright law.

Also this image is likely complex enough to be copyrightable. Here's a copyright appeal for your perusal: http://ipmall.info/sites/default/files/hosted_resources/CopyrightAppeals/2006/CCC%20Logo.pdf . Note that the "CCC Logo" was deemed below the threshold of originality but the "Car Credit City Logo" was not.

The "Car Credit City Logo" is roughly as complex as the image in this article (I'd argue the article image is more complex, actually).

And regardless of all this we still require sources anyway.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Sadly "pretty sure" isn't good enough. We need a source for this or Dave needs to claim that they made it.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty sure that's Dave's work so this is probably all moot, but the combination of a trademark-eligible graphic element and a distinctive phrase push this into the realm of a potentially copyright-eligible logo/placard/etc. Not certain it'd stand up in court, but it's enough that one could make a serious case for it.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: We can't verify that. Fonts can be copyrighted, so that will need sourced.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: I'm fairly certain that image is simple enough to fall below the threshold of originality, and thus not be copyrightable.

item.title: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Images used in posted articles must comply with the site's image policy. A source must be provided on the discussion page and the source must be compatible with the site's license. The author will be notified after this posting via Wikidot PM and will have 48 hours to properly source the image before it is edited out of the page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't help chuckling to myself what would happen in the resident evil movies, Home Alone, and Tangled. My only problem with this scip is that the addenda isn't two and a half pages long.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-096-j

item.title: Really good
item.content:encoded: Didn't have a problem with this and it was creative

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know what would be cool? If we had a tale explaining the relationship to this and SCP-2578-d. Maybe it'll be a huge thing with author avatars. I would love that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a good joke to put here later.
I would put the joke here now but the place I'm at is monitoring me and the joke is NSFW and I don't want to get "reprimanded."

But none the less daveyougenius you've done it again. +1

Is the joke here yet?
No...

Aww…
Edit: It is now three days later and I have forgotten said joke, if anyone has such a thing as a reverse amnestic please contact me, it was a good joke about this skip. I'm sad.

item.title: Re: Shouldn't this be a J scp?
item.content:encoded: Mainlist articles can be funny. Joke articles don't need to be the only funny articles. I don't think this needs to be a -J.

item.title: Re: Shouldn't this be a J scp?
item.content:encoded: It's certainly funny, but it also has a serious context behind the humor and it's not a big stretch to imagine that somebody might create an anomaly like this in-universe. I think the big difference with -Js is that they're generally far more exaggerated.

item.title: Shouldn't this be a J scp?
item.content:encoded: It just needs to be a J-SCP. Please. I love it so much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One thing that bothered me: In the addendum, the first part says that the "masters" are terminated by sniper fire. Then they are still alive in the next paragraph. Was that intentional and just supposed to be a surrealistic thing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very good SCP! The experiment logs were well-made and definitely improved the article, and the comical parts were actually funny, a rarity for me.

Anyhow, that's just what I thought. Definite +1 for a great SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hadn't thought about that idea… honestly, I have an answer for that question, but it probably isn't what you were expecting, and I want to maybe save it for a tale a little later. Thanks!
